a b s t r a c t
Gadolinium(III) complexes are widely employed as contrast agents in diagnostic magnetic resonance
imaging, and the development of new contrast agents remains a highly active ﬁeld. A key requisite for
contrast agents is high thermodynamic stability of the complex to ensure that the Gd(III) ion is not
released in the body. Here we utilized published stability constants spanning 20 orders of magnitude
to develop empirical expressions based on structural descriptors to predict the Gd(III)-ligand formation
constant as well as pGd at pH 7.4. We then tested the predictive power of these expressions and found
excellent agreement with a mean deviation of 1.0 log K units. The magnitudes of the structural descrip-
tors are useful for guiding ligand design for Gd(III), and the empirical stability constant expressions can
be used to screen potential ligands. This methodology is readily extended to other aqueous metal ion
systems.

pages.text:
due to the application of some of them as contrast agents in clinical
magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [1–6]. Commercially available
contrast agents are small Gd(III) chelates with octadentate ligands
that leave a coordination position available for a water ligand. The
coordinated water molecule is involved in chemical exchange with
the water of tissues and organs, which provides an efﬁcient mech-
anism for the shortening of longitudinal and transverse relaxation
times of water proton nuclei. This relaxation enhancement effect
can be used to increase the image contrast by applying adequate
pulse sequences [6].
A key aspect of the Gd(III) complexes used as contrast agents is
their stability, which must be high enough to prevent their disso-
ciation within the body and the release of the toxic metal ion.
While Gd(III)-based contrast agents are being used routinely in
clinical practice and biomedical research, some toxicity issues have
been identiﬁed [7]. In 2006, a potentially fatal disease named
nephrogenic systemic ﬁbrosis was associated with the administra-
tion of Gd(III) contrast agents to patients with renal impairment
[8,9]. More recently, increasing evidence for the accumulation
and retention of Gd(III) in the brain and other tissues has been
reported, although it is not clear whether the deposited metal
ion is still present as the chelate or in another chemical form
[10,11]. The biological ramiﬁcations of this retained Gd(III) are as
yet unknown [12,13].
There has been a major effort to improve the properties of the
commercially available contrast agents by introducing different
modiﬁcations to the ligand scaffold and developing innovative
ligand structures [14,15]. As a result, the coordination chemistry
of Gd(III) and the other lanthanide ions has experienced an enor-
mous development, thanks to the combined research effort of
many groups [1,16,17].
There is a wide consensus in the scientiﬁc community that the
integrity of Gd(III) complexes in vivo is related both to kinetic
inertness and thermodynamic stability [18]. Contrast agents must
present a high thermodynamic stability and, perhaps more impor-
tantly, they must be inert with respect to their dissociation [19].
While inertness is clearly an important factor when considering
the initial biological exposure to a contrast agent, thermodynamic
stability will play a major role when considering the long term
(months to years) fate of these complexes in the body, particularly
in the case of linear contrast agents. Thermodynamic stability data
of many Gd(III) complexes was accumulated during the course of
the last two decades, particularly for complexes structurally
related to the commercial contrast agents [Gd(DTPA)(H2O)]2
and [Gd(DOTA)(H2O)]. For these systems, a wide range of struc-
tural modiﬁcations were introduced to the ligand scaffold and
the impact of such modiﬁcations in complex stability was ana-
lyzed. These thermodynamic data allowed for the establishment
of some general principles for the design of stable Gd(III)
complexes.
Here we sought to use the large body of thermodynamic data to
develop a quantitative analysis of the thermodynamic stability
constants on the basis of structural descriptors. We show that
the thermodynamic stability of Gd(III) complexes can be predicted
to a good accuracy using an empirical expression relying on struc-
tural descriptors. This correlation provides a powerful tool to aid
ligand design and rationalize the different factors that determine
the stability of Gd(III) complexes in solution. To validate the
approach we applied this methodology to 12 new Gd(III) com-
plexes. A quantitative structure–property relationship based on a
machine-learning method was recently developed to predict the
thermodynamic stability constants of Gd(III) complexes (log KGdL)
[20]. This method relied on parametrized functions encoding the
2D structure of the ligand. Another attempt to correlate the struc-
tures of Gd(III) complexes and their stability constants used topo-
logical
and
quantum
chemical
descriptors
to
describe
the
structures of the ligands of a small data set of about 20 compounds
[21]. Our approach is based on structural descriptors that can be
easily identiﬁed, such as the number and type of donor groups
and the size of the macrocyclic platform, which represents a poten-
tially more chemically intuitive way to design new chelators.
2. Stability constants of Gd(III) complexes
2.1. Complexes with acyclic ligands
The stability constants of a rather large number of Gd(III) com-
plexes with acyclic ligands have been reported in the literature.
Particularly exhaustive was the investigation of H5DTPA and
H3DTPA-bis(amide) derivatives, which is clearly attributable to
the use of these complexes as contrast agents in clinical practice.
A ﬁrst set of complexes that has been investigated is comprised
by H5DTPA (A1 [22-24]) and derivatives containing different sub-
stituents at the central N atom (Fig. 1). The nature of the pendant
arm attached to the central N atom was varied to introduce an
a-substituted acetic acid arm (A2-A4) [25,26], alkyl groups
(A5, A6) [27,28], or methylphosphonic acid (A7, A8) [29], aceta-
mide (A9) [23,30] and methylpyridine functions (A10) [31]. Among
these substituents, only the incorporation of a methylphosphonic
acid group results in a complex with higher stability than
[Gd(DTPA)(H2O)]2 itself (Table 1).
Another group of H5DTPA derivatives contains two substituents
on the terminal N atoms that replace the acetic acid groups of the
former (Fig. 1). This family is largely dominated by H3DTPA-bis
(amide) ligands (A14-A23) [23,32–36], but ligands containing
a-substituted acetic acid (A11) [25], acetic acid ester (A12) [23],
alkyl (A13) [37], and methylpyridine (A24) groups [32], were also
investigated. In the case of bis(amide) derivatives the inspection of
the stability constants (Table 1) shows that ligands with primary
amide groups (A14) provide complexes with lower stability than
those containing secondary amides (A15-A20), while ligands con-
taining tertiary amide groups (A21-A23) show the highest stabil-
ity. The stability constants determined for H5DTPA (A1) and
H3DTPA–BMA (A16) using different ionic strengths do not differ
signiﬁcantly (Table 1).
A third group of H5DTPA derivatives contain a substituent other
than acetic acid on only one of the terminal N atoms (A25-A29,
Fig. 1) [22,23,30,38], or three substituents other than acetic acid
on each of the three N atoms of the H5DTPA scaffold (A30) [25].
The complex of A28 contains a propionate group replacing one of
the terminal acetic acid arms of H5DTPA, a substitution that
decreases the stability constant by more than 2 log K units [38].
Finally, the stability constants of H5DTPA derivatives containing a
C-functionalized scaffold, such as MS-325 (GdA31) and H5EOB-
DTPA
(A32)
have
been
also
reported
[39,40].
The
stability
constants of these complexes are very similar to that of the parent
H5DTPA complex, which indicates that C-functionalization has a
minor impact in complex stability.
The family of H5DTPA derivatives A1-A32 present stability con-
stants that cover a range of approximately eight orders of magni-
tude, with the complex of the bis(amide) derivative A14 showing
the lowest stability constant (log KGdL = 14.6), and that of A7, which
contains a methylphosphonic acid group, yielding the highest sta-
bility constant (log KGdL = 22.79) [29,33].
Besides H5DTPA derivatives, thermodynamic stability constants
of complexes with linear, structurally related ligands, have been
also reported. Some of these ligands are shown in Fig. 2, while
the corresponding stability constants are compiled in Table 1. This
group of ligands contain one or more of the following structural
modiﬁcations: 1) Propylene chains replacing one (A34, A35) or
two (A33, A38) of the ethylene bridges of H5DTPA derivatives

[67,68,72]; 2) Ether oxygen atoms instead of the amine N atoms of
the H5DTPA backbone (A36-A38, A40, A41) [42–44,71]; 3) A pyri-
dyl group replacing the central amine N atom (A39) [41]; 4) Phenyl
spacers instead of the ethylene bridges of H5DTPA (H4BAPTA, A41)
[44]; 5) Extended linear structures with a fourth donor atom in the
poly(oxo)amine
chain (A40-A42)
[45,71,72].
These
structural
alterations result in a broad range of thermodynamic stability
constants, the lowest being that of the complex of H4BAPTA
(A41, log KGdL = 10.6) [44], and the highest that of the H6TTHA
complex (A42, log KGdL = 28.4) [45]. This expands the range of
stability constants of H5DTPA derivatives to up to 18 orders of
magnitude.
Another important type of non-macrocyclic ligand is H4EDTA
(A43, Fig. 3) [46], and a wide family of derivatives that has been
extensively investigated. The central ethylenediamine unit has
been functionalized with different groups, including acetamides
(A44-A46) [47,48], methylpyridine (A47, A54) [49,55], phenol
(A49, A53) [50,54] or a hydroxyethyl group (A55) [56]. Ligands
with lower denticity in which some of the acetic acid groups are
either missing (A48) or are replaced by alkyl substituents (A51,
A52) can be also included in this group [46,52,53]. One of the sys-
tems (H4OCTAPA, A50) contains bidentate 6-methylpicolinic acid
groups [51], a binding motif that has been incorporated to a wide
range of macrocyclic ligands (see below). Different structural mod-
iﬁcations have been also incorporated to the H4EDTA scaffold
(Fig. 3), by substituting the ethylene linker by cyclohexyl (A56
(H4CDTA), A57 (H4CHXOCTAPA)) [46,57], propyl (A58 (H4TMDTA),
A59) [58], phenyl (A60, H4PhDTA) [59] or cyclobutyl (A69, A70,
A71) spacers [65]. Ligand A61 can be seen as a tripodal ligand,
but also as an H4EDTA derivative in which one of the N atoms is
replaced by an ether oxygen atom and two acetic acid pendant
arms are missing [25]. The stability constants of H4EDTA deriva-
tives also cover a wide range of almost 13 orders of magnitude.
The highest stability constant was reported for the complex with
the octadentate ligand A57 (H4CHXOCTAPA, log KGdL = 20.68)
[57], while the lowest corresponds to the complex with the
tetradentate ligand A61 (log KGdL = 7.88) [25].
A third group of non-macrocyclic ligands is the family of tripo-
dal ligands such as H3NTA (A62) and H2IDA (A63) [60,61], and
H2IDA derivatives containing alkyl (A64, A66) [25], hydroxyethyl
(A65) [62], or phenol (A67) substituents [63]. The simplest ligand
of this class considered in this work is glycine (A68) [64]. The
most stable complex among this series of tripodal ligands is pro-
vided by A67, which suggest the favorable properties of the phe-
nol group for stable Gd(III) complexation (Fig. 3). A class of
tripodal ligands that has been rather intensively investigated is
the
HOPO
family,
such
as
TREN-1-Me-3,2-HOPO
(A72)
and
TREN-6-Me-3,2-HOPO (A73) (Fig. 3). The Gd(III) complexes of
these ligands and closely related derivatives were found to con-
tain two or three water molecules coordinated to the metal ion,
which results in remarkably high relaxation efﬁciencies [73,74].
The thermodynamic stability of the Gd(III) complexes of some
HOPO derivatives was reported, though the amount of data avail-
able is scarce. The complexes of the parent HOPO ligands A72 and
A73 are characterized by relatively high stability constants of log
KGdL = 19.2 and 19.5, respectively [66]. This stability is enhanced
considerably by replacing one of the HOPO units by a terephtha-
lamide moiety, with the complexes of A74 and A75 being charac-
terized by log KGdL = 24.3 and 24.1, respectively [66,69]. Other
structural modiﬁcations introduced in one of the chains of the tri-
pod, to give A76-A78, provoke a signiﬁcant drop in complex sta-
bility [70].

blocks.text:
[67,68,72]; 2) Ether oxygen atoms instead of the amine N atoms of
the H5DTPA backbone (A36-A38, A40, A41) [42–44,71]; 3) A pyri-
dyl group replacing the central amine N atom (A39) [41]; 4) Phenyl
spacers instead of the ethylene bridges of H5DTPA (H4BAPTA, A41)
[44]; 5) Extended linear structures with a fourth donor atom in the
poly(oxo)amine
chain (A40-A42)
[45,71,72].
These
structural
alterations result in a broad range of thermodynamic stability
constants, the lowest being that of the complex of H4BAPTA
(A41, log KGdL = 10.6) [44], and the highest that of the H6TTHA
complex (A42, log KGdL = 28.4) [45]. This expands the range of
stability constants of H5DTPA derivatives to up to 18 orders of
magnitude.
Another important type of non-macrocyclic ligand is H4EDTA
(A43, Fig. 3) [46], and a wide family of derivatives that has been
extensively investigated. The central ethylenediamine unit has
been functionalized with different groups, including acetamides
(A44-A46) [47,48], methylpyridine (A47, A54) [49,55], phenol
(A49, A53) [50,54] or a hydroxyethyl group (A55) [56]. Ligands
with lower denticity in which some of the acetic acid groups are
either missing (A48) or are replaced by alkyl substituents (A51,
A52) can be also included in this group [46,52,53]. One of the sys-
tems (H4OCTAPA, A50) contains bidentate 6-methylpicolinic acid
groups [51], a binding motif that has been incorporated to a wide
range of macrocyclic ligands (see below). Different structural mod-
iﬁcations have been also incorporated to the H4EDTA scaffold
(Fig. 3), by substituting the ethylene linker by cyclohexyl (A56
(H4CDTA), A57 (H4CHXOCTAPA)) [46,57], propyl (A58 (H4TMDTA),
A59) [58], phenyl (A60, H4PhDTA) [59] or cyclobutyl (A69, A70,
A71) spacers [65]. Ligand A61 can be seen as a tripodal ligand,
but also as an H4EDTA derivative in which one of the N atoms is
replaced by an ether oxygen atom and two acetic acid pendant

arms are missing [25]. The stability constants of H4EDTA deriva-
tives also cover a wide range of almost 13 orders of magnitude.
The highest stability constant was reported for the complex with
the octadentate ligand A57 (H4CHXOCTAPA, log KGdL = 20.68)
[57], while the lowest corresponds to the complex with the
tetradentate ligand A61 (log KGdL = 7.88) [25].
A third group of non-macrocyclic ligands is the family of tripo-
dal ligands such as H3NTA (A62) and H2IDA (A63) [60,61], and
H2IDA derivatives containing alkyl (A64, A66) [25], hydroxyethyl
(A65) [62], or phenol (A67) substituents [63]. The simplest ligand
of this class considered in this work is glycine (A68) [64]. The
most stable complex among this series of tripodal ligands is pro-
vided by A67, which suggest the favorable properties of the phe-
nol group for stable Gd(III) complexation (Fig. 3). A class of
tripodal ligands that has been rather intensively investigated is
the
HOPO
family,
such
as
TREN-1-Me-3,2-HOPO
(A72)
and
TREN-6-Me-3,2-HOPO (A73) (Fig. 3). The Gd(III) complexes of
these ligands and closely related derivatives were found to con-
tain two or three water molecules coordinated to the metal ion,
which results in remarkably high relaxation efﬁciencies [73,74].
The thermodynamic stability of the Gd(III) complexes of some
HOPO derivatives was reported, though the amount of data avail-
able is scarce. The complexes of the parent HOPO ligands A72 and
A73 are characterized by relatively high stability constants of log
KGdL = 19.2 and 19.5, respectively [66]. This stability is enhanced
considerably by replacing one of the HOPO units by a terephtha-
lamide moiety, with the complexes of A74 and A75 being charac-
terized by log KGdL = 24.3 and 24.1, respectively [66,69]. Other
structural modiﬁcations introduced in one of the chains of the tri-
pod, to give A76-A78, provoke a signiﬁcant drop in complex sta-
bility [70].

stable Gd(III) complexes that are often very inert with respect to
their dissociation. The CYCLEN-based backbone functionalized
with four acetic acid pendant arms (H4DOTA, B1, Fig. 4) forms a
very stable and inert Gd(III) complex that is widely used as a
MRI contrast agent in clinical practice (gadoterate meglumine)
[75]. The CYCLEN platform has been also functionalized with four
identical pendant arms replacing the acetic acid arms of H4DOTA.
These pendant arms include 2-propionic acid (B2, H4DOTMA)
[76], acetamides (B5-B8) [77], methylphosphonic acid (B9, H8-
DOTP) [78], methylphosphinic acid (B10 (H4DOTPEt); B11 (H4-
DOTPMeOH)) [79,80], and methylphosphonic acid esters (B12
(H4DOTPOEt), B13 (H4DOTPOBu)) [80,81]. Some closely related
derivatives containing substituents on one carbon atom of the
CYCLEN ring have been also investigated (B3, B4) [82,83]. The sta-
bility constants reported for these two closely related derivatives
differ by 2.7 log K units, with that of [Gd(DOTA)(H2O)] falling in
between them (Table 2). The stability constants reported for the
Gd(III) complexes of CYCLEN-based ligands containing four identi-
cal pendant arms vary dramatically depending on the nature of the
side chains (Table 2). The most stable complex of this series is that
with H8DOTP (B9, log KGdL = 28.8) [78], while its n-butyl ester
derivative H4DOTPOBu presents a stability constant 16.6 log K units
lower (B13 (H4DOTPOBu), log KGdL = 12.19) [81]. As observed for H3-
DTPA-bis(amide) derivatives, the CYCLEN-based ligand containing
four acetamide pendants DOTAM (B5) forms a complex with a
lower stability compared with the analogues containing substi-
tuted acetamides B6-B8 [77].
Stability constants were also determined for complexes of sev-
eral H3DO3A derivatives. The stability constants of the complex
with H3DO3A (B14, Fig. 4) determined using ionic strengths of
0.1 M KCl (log KGdL = 21.56) and 0.15 M NaCl (log KGdL = 19.06,
Table 2) differ considerably [84,85]. A similar effect is observed
comparing the stability constants determined for the complexes
of the monoamide derivatives B16 and B17. The stability constant
of the complex with B17 was determined in 0.1 M NaCl, and found
to be 2.2 log K units lower than that of B16 determined in 0.1 M
KCl [86,87]. These data indicate that these ligands form a rather
stable complex with Na+ that competes with the Gd(III) ion, result-
ing in lower values of the stability constants. The stability constant
of the Na+ complex of DOTA was estimated to be log KNaL = 4.2 [88].
As a result, the basicity of CYCLEN derivatives varies considerably
depending on the cation present in the background salt. For
instance, the ﬁrst protonation constant of DOTP8 was determined
to be log K1 = 13.7, 12.1 and 10.9 in 0.1 M Me4N(NO3), KNO3 and
NaCl [89]. Thus, stability constants measured using different ionic
strengths cannot be directly compared. This is in sharp contrast
with the thermodynamic data reported for H5DTPA derivatives,
which do not differ signiﬁcantly in different ionic strengths (i. e.
log KGdL values of 22.03, 22.26 and 22.46 were reported for
GdDTPA2 using I = 0.15 M NaCl, 0.1 M NaCl and 0.1 M KNO3,
respectively, Table 1).
In addition to acetamide groups, the H3DO3A platform was also
functionalized with picolinic acid (B15) [88], a-substituted acetic
acid (B18) [90], methylphosphonic acid (B22) [91], or methylphos-
phinic acid groups (B23) [92]. The stability constants of H3DO3A
ligands containing hydroxyethyl (B19), 2-hydroxypropyl (B20), or
3-hydroxypropyl (B21) pendants suggest that the presence of the

Table 2) differ considerably [84,85]. A similar effect is observed
comparing the stability constants determined for the complexes
of the monoamide derivatives B16 and B17. The stability constant
of the complex with B17 was determined in 0.1 M NaCl, and found
to be 2.2 log K units lower than that of B16 determined in 0.1 M
KCl [86,87]. These data indicate that these ligands form a rather
stable complex with Na+ that competes with the Gd(III) ion, result-
ing in lower values of the stability constants. The stability constant
of the Na+ complex of DOTA was estimated to be log KNaL = 4.2 [88].
As a result, the basicity of CYCLEN derivatives varies considerably
depending on the cation present in the background salt. For
instance, the ﬁrst protonation constant of DOTP8 was determined
to be log K1 = 13.7, 12.1 and 10.9 in 0.1 M Me4N(NO3), KNO3 and
NaCl [89]. Thus, stability constants measured using different ionic
strengths cannot be directly compared. This is in sharp contrast
with the thermodynamic data reported for H5DTPA derivatives,
which do not differ signiﬁcantly in different ionic strengths (i. e.
log KGdL values of 22.03, 22.26 and 22.46 were reported for
GdDTPA2 using I = 0.15 M NaCl, 0.1 M NaCl and 0.1 M KNO3,
respectively, Table 1).
In addition to acetamide groups, the H3DO3A platform was also
functionalized with picolinic acid (B15) [88], a-substituted acetic
acid (B18) [90], methylphosphonic acid (B22) [91], or methylphos-
phinic acid groups (B23) [92]. The stability constants of H3DO3A
ligands containing hydroxyethyl (B19), 2-hydroxypropyl (B20), or
3-hydroxypropyl (B21) pendants suggest that the presence of the

The stability constants reported in the literature for the com-
plex of H2DO2A (B24) differ by up to 6 orders of magnitude. Two
independent determinations yielded very similar stability con-
stants (log KGdL = 19.1 and 19.42) [94,95], while a much lower
value was determined by Chang (log KGdL = 13.06) [96]. These dis-
crepancies are likely related to the slow kinetics of formation of
this complex. A value of log KGdL of about 19 appears to be more
consistent with the stability decrease observed upon removing
one of the acetic acid pendant arms of H4DOTA (log KGdL = 24.7
for [Gd(DOTA)(H2O)]) to give H3DO3A (log KGdL = 21.6), which
suggests that each carboxylate provides a contribution of about 3
log K units. Thus, one would expect a stability constant for the
H2DO2A derivative of log KGdL  18.6, a value that further suggests
that the stability constant reported by Chang is incorrect [96]. We
thus retained in our analysis a stability constant of log KGdL = 19.1
(Table 2). The stabilities of the complexes with the H2DO2A deriva-
tives B26 (H2HE-DO2A) and B27 (H2HP-DO2A, Fig. 4, Table 2) con-
ﬁrm that ligands containing 2-hydroxypropyl groups form more
stable complexes than the hydroxyethyl analogues (B26) [94].
The stability constant reported for the complex of H6DO2A2P
(B25, Table 2) is intermediate between those of H4DOTA and H8-
DOTP, suggesting a higher contribution to complex stability of
the methylphosphononic acid compared with acetic acid arms
[97]. A relatively large variety of asymmetrically-substituted
CYCLEN derivatives, containing different types of pendant arms,
were also used for Gd(III) complexation (Fig. 4, B28-B33) [98–100].
A structural modiﬁcation of the CYCLEN platform that was
investigated in detail is the incorporation of pyridine units to the
macrocyclic scaffold (Fig. 5). The best known example of this class
of ligands is the triacetic acid derivative H3PCTA (B34, Fig. 5). As for
CYCLEN derivatives, the stability constants of [Gd(PCTA)(H2O)2]
determined in 0.1 M KCl and 0.15 M NaCl differ considerably (more
than 2 log K units) [102,124]. Other derivatives whose stability
constants were determined are the bis(acetic) acid derivative
H2PC2A (B36) [95], as well as systems containing mixed acetic acid
and picolinic acid pendant arms (B37-B40) [102,103]. These pen-
dant arms were also incorporated to the 12-membered macrocycle
containing two pyridyl units in the macrocyclic backbone (B41-
B42) [95,104]. Additionally, structural modiﬁcations such as the
incorporation of a C-alkyl group to H3PCTA (B35) or replacing ethyl
by propyl chains in the macrocyclic scafold (B43) were also
explored
[101,105].
The
same
structural
modiﬁcation
was
introduced to H4DOTA, yielding the 13 and 14 membered macrocy-
cles H4TRITA (B44, Fig. 5) and H4TETA (B45) [106]. The stabilities of
complexes with 12-membered macrocycles in which amine N
atoms were replaced by ether O atoms were also investigated
(B46 and B47, Fig. 5) [107,108].
The stability constants reported for [Gd(PCTA)(H2O)2] (H3-
PCTA = B34) are ca. 1 log K unit lower than those of [Gd(DO3A)
(H2O)2] (H3DO3A = B14, Table 2) determined using the same ionic
strength, suggesting that the incorporation of a pyridine ring to the
macrocyclic scaffold has a negative contribution to the stability
constant (Table 2). However, the rigidifying effect introduced by
the pyridyl ring was proved to enhance the kinetic inertness of
the complex [124]. The comparison of the stability constants of
the complexes with H3DO3A (B14) and H3ODOTRA (B47) suggests
that replacing a amine nitrogen atom by an ether oxygen atom has
a minor impact in complex stability.
The only bidentate chelating unit that was incorporated to dif-
ferent macrocyclic platforms is the 6-methylpicolinic acid group.
In some instances the presence of the picolinic acid group provided
an additional stability to the Gd(III) complex compared to the cor-
responding derivative containing acetic acid pendant arms. This is
for instance the case of the complexes with B37 and B38, which
present log KGdL values of 22.47 and 20.39 (I = 0.15 M NaCl)
[102]. These stability constants are clearly higher than that deter-
mined for the H3PCTA (B34) complex under the same conditions
(log KGdL = 18.28) [102]. However, it is also noteworthy that the
complexes of B37 and B38 present stability constants that differ
by 2 log K units in spite of having identical donor sets. This indi-
cates that steric effects are likely playing a role in decreasing the
stability of the complex with B38. Replacing an additional acetic
acid group of B37 and B38 by a picolinic acid pendant arm further
increases the stability of the corresponding Gd(III) complexes (log
KGdL = 23.44 and 23.56 for GdB39 and GdB40, respectively) [103].
The steric hindrance associated to the coordination of the biden-
tate picoliante unit is particularly evident for CYCLEN-based sys-
tems. Indeed, the stability constant determined for the complex
of B15 (log KGdL = 23.31) [88], which contains a picolinate arm
replacing one of the acetate groups of [Gd(DOTA)(H2O)], is clearly
lower than that of the latter (log KGdL = 24.7) [75]. Additional
examples are provided by complexes GdB30 and GdB31, which
present stability constants lower than that of the parent [Gd
(DO3A)(H2O)2] derivative (GdB14) recorded under the same condi-
tions (Table 2) [85,100].

macrocycles is unfortunately quite scarce. A few triazacyclononane
(TACN) derivatives containing acetic acid, picolinic acid, propionic
acid or methylphosphonic acid pendant arms have been described
and the stability constants of the Gd(III) complexes determined
(C1-C5, Fig. 6) [109–112]. The family of 18-membered macrocycles
includes H2DACDA (D1) and the corresponding picolinic acid
derivative H2MACROPA (D2), which was shown to be very selective
for large metal ions [113,114]. The analogue of H2DACDA contain-
ing propionic acid pendant arms (D3) and the monocarboxylic acid
derivative D4 were also investigated [115]. Besides these 1,10-
diaza-18-crown-6 derivatives, stability data were also reported
for the triacetic acid derivative of 1,7,13-triaza-18-crown-6 D5
and the tetraacetic acid derivative of a 18-membered macrocycle
containing pyridyl head units H4PYTA (D6) [108,116,108,116].
Concerning the stability of complexes with 15-membered macro-
cycles, only four examples have been investigated (E1-E4, Fig. 6)
[108,117–119].
A class of ligands that was developped especiﬁcally for forma-
tion of stable Gd(III) complexes is the H4AAZTA family, which are
mesocyclic ligands based on a 6-amino-6-methylperhydro-1,4-dia
zepine platform (Fig. 6) [125]. While these ligands are not macro-
cyclic, strictly speaking, they show properties that are closer to
macrocyclic systems, and thus are discussed here. The thermody-
namic data available in the literature for Gd(III)-AAZTA derivatives
is scarce, with only a few stability constants reported. The stability
of the Gd(III) complex with H4AAZTA (F1, Fig. 6) determined using
0.1 M KCl and 0.15 M NaCl backgrounds differ by 1.3 log K units
(Table 2), a behavior that parallels CYCLEN derivatives and implies
afﬁnity for the Na+ ion. The presence of non-coordinating C-alkyl
groups in F2 and F3 causes a slight increase in complex stability,
which was attributed to an increased ligand basicity [121]. Rigidi-
ﬁcation of the H4AAZTA structure with a cyclohexyl ring resulted in
a decrease in the stability constant determined using the same
ionic strenght (2 log K units) [122].

A recent study reported the stability constants of the Gd(III)
complexes with the regioisomeric ligands F5 and F6, which contain
a phenol group either at the exocyclic N atom of the H4AAZTA sca-
fold (F5) or on one of the endocyclic N atoms (F6). The complex
with F5 is characterized by a particularly high stability constant
(log KGdL = 25.06), which is 5.5 log K units higher than that of the
F6 derivative. This atonishing difference was explained by steric
effects that hinder the coordination of the bulky phenol group
attached to an endocyclic N atom [123].
3. Analysis of complex stability using structural descriptors
3.1. Estimation of stability constants
The log KGdL values described in the previous section cover a
wide range of values from 3.7 for the glycinate complex to 28.8
for the complex of H8DOTP (Fig. 7). For 85% of the macrocyclic sys-
tems the stability constant values (60) fall within the range
10 < log KGdL < 24. Up to 76% (74) of the log KGdL values of com-
plexes with acyclic ligands are within the 10 < logKGdL < 24 range,
with only three values above this range (H6TTHA (A42) and the
HOPO derivatives A74 and A75, Table 1).
Given the rather large number of Gd(III) complexes whose sta-
bility was investigated, we postulated that these data could be
used to obtain an empirical expression allowing to predict and
rationalize the stability constants. We hypothesized that the stabil-
ity constants could be approximated by an expression of the form:

cyclohehyl (SCyhx), cyclobutyl (SCybu) or phenyl (SPh) group,
and substituting an amine nitrogen atom by an ether oxygen atom
(SOe) or a pyridyl ring (SPy). For instance, H4CDTA (A56, Fig. 3) is
described by nN = 2 + nC = 4 + nSCyhx = 1, while H4EGTA is deﬁned
with nN = 4 + nC = 4 + nSOe = 2 (two ether oxygen atoms are
replacing two of the four amine nitrogen atoms). Note that while
the Dlog Ki terms must be positive, since all donor groups should
have a certain contribution to complex stability, the Dlog Kj values
might be positive or negative depending on the effect of such
structural modiﬁcation. Inspection of the stability constants shows
that C-functionalization of the ligand scaffold does not affect sig-
niﬁcantly the stability constants, and thus this structural modiﬁca-
tion was not considered.
For the description of the complexes with macrocyclic ligands
we had to use a slightly different approach to account for the
intrinsic coordination properties of the macrocycle, in terms not
only of number (and type) of donor atoms, but also the size match
between the macrocycle and the metal ion. Thus, for macrocyclic
ligands we used a term AGdL to capture the contribution to the sta-
bility constant of the macrocyclic unit, while the ﬁrst summation
described the contributions of the donor groups of the pendant
arms only, and the second summation the structural modiﬁcations
of the macrocyclic ring and pendant arms.
Following the qualitative observations described above, we
have used two different Dlog KPic values as ﬁtting parameters, to
account for sterically hindered (Dlog KPic*) and non-hindered
picolinate groups. The criterion followed to choose one or another
parameter was the comparison of the stability constants of the car-
boxylate and picolinate analogues. A sterically hindered picolinate
was considered in those cases in which the stability of the carboxy-
late derivative is higher than that of the picolinate analogue (i. e.
B1 and B15). Concerning the effect of the ionic strength, in the case
of complexes with macrocyclic ligands derived from CYCLEN and
structural modiﬁcations, we used two different parameters A as ﬁt-
ting parameters to account for ionic strengths adjusted with NaCl
or other electrolyte.
An expression such as that deﬁned in [1] yields higher calcu-
lated stability constants on increasing the denticity of the ligand.
However, the Gd(III) ion rarely exceeds coordination number nine
in aqueous solution, reaching coordination number ten in some
cases [126]. All ligands described in the previous section are non-
adentate with the exceptions of A42 (H6TTHA), D2 (H2MACROPA)
and D6 (H4PYTA). The crystal structures of TTHA6 complexes with
the larger Ln(III) ions show the metal ion coordinated in a decaden-
tate fashion, while complexes of the smaller Ln(III) ions show
9-fold
coordination
[127,128].
The
Gd(III)
complexes
of

shown

[114,129].
A least squares ﬁt of the stability constants reported for the 133
complexes (142 stability constant values) with the ligands shown
in Figs. 1–6 provided calculated stability constants in excellent
agreement with the experimental values, as evidenced in Fig. 7
(R2 = 0.997). The least-squares ﬁt involved 26 ﬁtting parameters:
The Dlog Ki of 16 donor groups, 5 Dlog Kj parameters describing
the different structural modiﬁcations and 5 AGdL parameters (1
for the 9, 15 and 18 membered macrocycles and 2 for the 12-
membered macrocycles). The average mean deviation of the calcu-
lated values with respect to the experimental ones is 0.75 log K
units, with the largest deviations being observed for the complexes

have used two different Dlog KPic values as ﬁtting parameters, to

A least squares ﬁt of the stability constants reported for the 133

agreement with the experimental values, as evidenced in Fig. 7

The Dlog Ki of 16 donor groups, 5 Dlog Kj parameters describing
the different structural modiﬁcations and 5 AGdL parameters (1

units, with the largest deviations being observed for the complexes
of H4BAPTA (A41), H2DO2A (B24), HP-DO3A-3HM (B33) and

DO2A complex (16.85) is somewhat lower than the experimental
value of 19.1 reported by Sherry [94], but conﬁrms that the stabil-
ity constant determined by Chang (log KGdL = 13.06) is likely wrong
[96]. The high stability constant of H2DO2A is likely related to a
lower steric hindrance associated to the coordination of the ligand,
as the acetic acid arms occupy opposite positions in the CYCLEN
framework. The ligands of the HOPO and AAZTA families were
not included in the analysis because the limited number of stability
constants reported in the literature prevented the estimation of
robust structural descriptors.
The accuracy of the predicted stability constants is remarkable,
considering: 1) The errors associated to the determination of the
experimental stability constants and the effect of using different
ionic strengths. For instance, log KGdL values differing by up to
2.2 log K units (in the range 20.7 to 23.0, 25 C) were reported
for [Gd(DTPA)(H2O)]2 using ionic strengths adjusted to 0.1 M with
different electrolytes [130]. The discrepancies are even more obvi-
ous for complexes with macrocyclic ligands, which might be
related to a more important effect of the electrolytes used in the
determination and the slow complexation kinetics often observed
for complexes with macrocyclic ligands. This is clearly illustrated
by the stability constants reported for [Gd(DOTA)(H2O)], which
differ by up to 5.9 log K units [130]; 2) Our approach to calculate
the stability constants is based on structural descriptors, which
consider only the number and type of donor groups and a series
of structural modiﬁcations that can be introduced in the ligand
scaffold. However, the arrangement of the donor atoms of the
ligand around the metal ion may also have an important impact
in complex stability as a result of steric interactions [100]. This
effect is clearly observed when comparing the stabilities of the
complexes with B37 and B38, which contain the same number
and type of pendant groups arranged in a different manner on
the macrocyclic platform. However, the corresponding stability
constants (log KGdL = 22.37 and 20.49, 0.15 M NaCl, 25 C) differ
by 1.9 log K units [102].

3.2. Estimation of pGd
The values of log KGdL do not take into account the basicity of
the ligand, as an increased ligand basicity generally increses the
log K values, but also reduces the stability constant of the complex
at physiological pH due to a more efﬁcient proton competition.
Indeed, different authors observed linear relationships between
the log KGdL values and the overall ligand basicity expressed as
the sum of the ligand protonation constants Plog Ki, generally
considering only structurally related ligands [94,131]. The large
set of data analyzed here display a relatively weak linear correla-
tion (R2 = 0.59), as demonstrated by the Pearson’s correlation coef-
ﬁcient of 0.77 (Fig. 8). Nevertheless, it is evident that ligand
basicity must be taken into account to assess complex stability
under physiologically relevant conditions.
One method to take into account the competition with acid is to
calculate the conditional stability constant at a given pH value, e.g.
pH 7.4. A related method is to calculate the pGd value at a given pH
under standardized conditions, deﬁned here as –log[Gd(III)]free at
pH 7.4 for a total Gd(III) concentration of 106 M and a ligand con-
centration of 105 M [132]. This is the most common deﬁnition
used in the literature, though it is evident that MRI contrast agents
are thermodynamically very stable and are not injected using a 10-
fold ligand excess. Thus, these pGd values should not be considered
as a measure of the actual free Gd(III) present in vivo if thermody-
namic equilibirum was reached, but rather a tool to compare the
stabilities of complexes with ligands having different basicities.
The lowest value of pGd according to the deﬁnition provided above
is pGd = 6, a situation in which all Gd(III) is in the uncomplexed
form at pH 7.4. The higher the pGd value, the more stable the com-
plex at pH 7.4. The values of pGd cover a narrower range of
6 < pGd < 24 compared to the log KGdL values (Fig. 9). Noteworthy
is the fact that complexes with macrocyclic and acyclic ligands
show similar distributions, with most complexes falling in the
range 11 < pGd < 20, and a few examples with pGd > 19. Thus,
the higher log KGdL values often observed for macrocyclic com-
plexes are not necessarily reﬂected in the corresponding pGd val-
ues, an effect that can be explained by the high basicity of many
macrocyclic ligands, particularly those derived from CYCLEN.
Given that Eq [1] predicts the stability constants of Gd(III) com-
plexes to a rather good accuracy, we next assessed whether a sim-
ilar expression can be used to predict pGd values. Thus, we
performed a least squares ﬁt of the available pGd values according
to the following expression:
pGd ¼

tributions to pGd of the different donor groups and structural mod-
iﬁcations, respectively, and ApGd is a parameter that characterizes
the contribution to pGd of the different macrocyclic platforms
depending on their size. The ﬁt of the data included 132 pGd val-
ues, a number that is somewhat lower than the one used to analyse
logKGdL data. This is explained by the lack of reported ligand proto-
nation constants determined under the same conditions as the log
KGdL values. Equation [2] gives calculated pGd values in reasonably
good agreement with those obtained from the experimental equi-
librium constants (R2 = 0.996, Fig. 9), though the agreement is not
as good as for logKGdL data. The largest deviations (2.5 pGd units)
are observed for H2DO2A (B24) and H8DOTP (B9), with only ﬁve
values presenting deviations > 2.0 pGd units, and an average abso-
lute deviation of 0.72 pGd units. The particularly large deviation
observed for the logKGdL and pGd values of H2DO2A suggests that
the experimental data must be treated with some caution and
probably reassessed.

groups (Fig. 10). That is, these neutral donors contribute to com-

basicity results in a rather low contribution to stability at pH 7.4.
Concerning the values of Dlog Kj and DpGdj, replacing an
ethylenediamine spacer by a propylenediamine group is clearly
detrimental for both logK and pGd, as judged by the very negative
Dlog Kj and DpGdj contributions. This is in line with earlier obser-
vations reported by Hancock, which demonstrated that large metal
ions, like Gd(III), prefer ﬁve-membered chelate rings, while small
metal ions form more stable six-membered chelate rings [135].
The incorporation of a phenyl spacer is characterized by a very
negative Dlog Kj value (–3.62) and also a negative DpGdj contribu-
tion (–1.84). The lower destabilizing contribution observed for pGd
is again related to the lower basicity of anilines compared to ali-
phatic amines [136]. Similarly, the low basicity of ether oxygen
atoms and pyridyl N atoms with respect to amine N atoms pro-
vokes a change from slightly negative Dlog Kj values to positive
DpGdj parameters. Thus, while replacing amine N atoms by ether
or pyridyl groups generally has a minor impact in terms of stability
constants, it usually results in increased pGd values. This is illus-
trated in Fig. 11, which presents the pGd values determined for a
series of structuraly related ligands in which amine N atoms are
replaced by ether or pyridyl groups. The fact that DpGdj < DlogKj
for a cyclohexyl substituent also correlates with the higher basicity
of ligands incorporating a cyclohexyl group instead of an ethyl
spacer (i. e. A50, H4OCTAPA, and A57, H4CHXOCTAPA) [57]. Con-
cerning the ApGd values, 12-, 15- and 18-membered macrocycles
are characterized by positive contributions, while 9-membered
macrocycles are characterized by negative values of ApGd. Interest-
ingly, the Gd(III) complex of the 18-membered macrocycle D6 (H4-
PYTA) is characterized by a high stability constant (log KGdL = 21.7)
and a high pGd value. However, structural modiﬁcations of this
platform aimed to increase complex stability were not developed
so far. Our model suggests that stabilities as high as log KGdL = 25.0
and a pGd value of 19.3 could be attained with the tetraphospho-
nate derivative, if coordination number 10 is retained. Thus, this
macrocyclic platform appears to be a very attractive candidate to
develop stable complexes with Gd(III) and other metal ions.
5. Validation of the model using a test set
To validate the structural descriptors described in the previous
sections, we determined the stability constants of a series of
complexes whose stability was either not reported before (T1-T4)
or were identiﬁed to be incorrectly reported during the course of
our analysis (T5-T6, Fig. 12). Indeed, the stability constants
reported in the literature for T5 (log KGdL = 10.6) and T6 (log
KGdL = 10.2) are 6–7 orders of magnitude lower than those pre-
dicted by our model (log KGdL = 15.91) and T6 (log KGdL = 17.45)
[137,138]. The protonation constants of these ligands and the cor-
responding stability constants and pGd values are listed in Table 4.
Furthermore, we incorporated into the test set a number of com-
plexes that were reported very recently (Fig. 12) [139–144].
The calculated log KGdL show a mean deviation of 1.0 log K units,
with the largest deviation being observerd for T10 (2.4 log K units).
We note that the values predicted for T1-T4 are all somewhat
higher than the experimental ones, which suggests that the
0.15 M ionic strength used for stability constant determination
may cause a slight depression of the log KGdL values. The mean
deviation of the calculated pGd values is 1.00 pGd units.
The stability constants predicted for the complexes of T5 and T6
present an excellent agreement with the values determined using
spectrophotometric titrations, conﬁrming that the literature values
obtained with pH-potentiometry were wrong. The low basicity of
these ligands evidenced by their protonation constants (Table 4)
results in a negligible complex dissociation at pH 2. pH potentiom-
etry experiments can only access equilibrium processes occurring
in the range 2 < pH < 13, and thus, reliable stability constants for
these systems cannot be obtained with this method. This high-
lights the importance of using alternative experimental methods
to conﬁrm the model used to ﬁt potentiometric data. The correla-
tion proposed here allows estimating the stability constants and
thus the pH range in which complex dissociation is expected,
therefore aiding experiment design. The stability constant of the

6. Conclusions
The use of Gd(III) complexes as MRI contrast agents triggered a
great amount of work to determine the thermodynamic stabilities
of complexes with this metal ion. The large body of data available
in the literature provides a unique opportunity to establish
structure-stability correlations. It is generally accepted by the
MRI community that kinetic inertness is more important than ther-
modynamic stability. However, thermodynamic stability may be
relevant if the lifetime of the agent in the body is comparable to
its dissociation half-life. Furthermore, other applications in which
stable lanthanide complexation is required may beneﬁt from the
analysis presented here, which provides a straightforward tool to
predict the stability constants of Gd(III) complexes and their pGd
values that was obtained from the analysis of literature data. The
empirical relations presented here are remarkably accurate despite
known differences in experimental stability constants arising from
the use of different ionic media and ionic strengths. Our approach
serves to identify published stability constant data that may be
incorrect. It also allows the prediction of the stability of de novo
Gd(III) complexes. Here we see value in using the calculated stabil-
ity constant to rule out synthesis of likely inferior complexes.
Another feature of this approach is the quantiﬁcation of different
structural parameters like donor atom or chelate ring type. While
certain changes like increasing chelate ring size from 5 to 6 is well
known to be detrimental to complex stability for large ions like Gd
(III), the current quantitative analysis shows how large this effect is
and how it may compare to other structural changes. We hope that
these quantitative descriptors will be useful to the novice in under-
standing this ﬁeld.
The comparison of descriptors for log KGdL and pGd is also infor-
mative and helpful in separating out contributions of ligand basic-
ity to log KGdL. For instance many of the CYCLEN-based ligands
have higher log KGdL values than H5DTPA-based ligands with com-
parable donor sets, however the pGd analysis reveals that these
higher log KGdL values arise from the much higher basicity of
CYCLEN and that at pH 7.4 the stability of the H5DTPA derivative
may be higher. Similarly contributions to complex stability from
low basicity donors like ether O or pyridyl N become apparent
when comparing pGd values and comparing differences between
the descriptors for log KGdL vs pGd.
Here we focused on Gd(III) complexes and stability at pH 7.4.
However this analysis can be readily extended in other directions.
For example the same approach could be applied to other metal
complexes in aqueous solution where thermodynamic stability is
a key driver, e.g. radiometal applications for imaging or therapy
with isotopes of Tb(III), Lu(III), Y(III), Ga(III), In(III), and Cu(II)
[146,147], or for alternative MRI contrast agents based on Mn(II)
or Fe(III) [148–151]. Another application is in the design of ligands
to selectively chelate one metal compared to others, where com-
parison of the structural descriptors for one metal ion compared
to another would be expected to provide guidance in ligand design
that maximizes differences in stability. For such an application a
lower pH to assess the pM value may also be informative.
